# Cross-dataset evaluation — CIC-IDS2017 → CIC-IDS2018

**Run date:** 2026-04-22
**Model under test:** `xgboost.joblib`, trained on 50 000 stratified
CIC-IDS2017 flows (`random_state=42`).
**In-distribution baseline:** weighted F1 **0.9983** on CIC-IDS2017 test split
([`results/cicids2017/metrics.json`](../results/cicids2017/metrics.json)).
**This document measures the same model on CIC-IDS2018**, generated by the
same CICFlowMeter family but on a different network the following year.

## Why this evaluation exists

The real-world PCAP eval ([`docs/real_world_eval.md`](real_world_eval.md))
showed catastrophic detection failure (binary F1 → 0.0) on Stratosphere
captures. Two competing hypotheses:

1. **Extractor drift.** Our Python `flow_extractor.py` produces features
   that differ subtly from Java CICFlowMeter, breaking the model.
2. **Distribution shift.** The model overfits to CIC-IDS2017's specific
   network topology, attack tooling, and traffic mix — and fails on any
   other network even with the *same* feature extractor.

CIC-IDS2018 isolates hypothesis #2: it is generated by the **same
CICFlowMeter** (Java), so feature semantics are preserved. If the model
generalises, it should achieve high F1 here. If it fails, distribution
shift — not extractor drift — is the dominant cause.

## Data

Two days from CIC-IDS2018, downloaded from the public AWS bucket
`s3://cse-cic-ids2018/` over HTTPS. No authentication required.

| Day | Bytes | Flows | Mapped labels (after dropping unknown classes) |
|-----|------:|------:|------------------------------------------------|
| `Thursday-01-03-2018.csv` | 107 842 858 | 331 100 | BENIGN: 238 037 / Infiltration: 93 063 |
| `Wednesday-14-02-2018.csv` | 358 223 333 | 1 048 575 | BENIGN: 667 626 / FTP-Patator: 193 360 / SSH-Patator: 187 589 |

**Label normalisation.** CIC-IDS2018 uses different label strings:
`Benign` → `BENIGN`, `Infilteration` (typo in original) → `Infiltration`,
`FTP-BruteForce` → `FTP-Patator`, `SSH-Bruteforce` → `SSH-Patator`,
`DoS attacks-Hulk` → `DoS Hulk`, etc. Full mapping in
[`scripts/eval_cross_dataset.py`](../scripts/eval_cross_dataset.py).

**Column normalisation.** CIC-IDS2018 uses abbreviated column names
(`Tot Fwd Pkts` instead of `Total Fwd Packets`, etc.). The script renames
all 70 features back to CIC-IDS2017 conventions before inference. The only
training feature absent from CIC-IDS2018 is the duplicate
`Fwd Header Length.1` — we synthesise it from `Fwd Header Length` (zero if
missing). Reproduce via:

```bash
python scripts/eval_cross_dataset.py --csv data/cicids2018/Thursday-01-03-2018.csv --out results/cross_dataset_thursday_infiltration.json
python scripts/eval_cross_dataset.py --csv data/cicids2018/Wednesday-14-02-2018.csv --out results/cross_dataset_wednesday_brute.json
```

Inference throughput: ~40 000 flows/sec on a single XGBoost CPU thread
(both runs combined: 1.38 M flows in 33 s).

## Headline result

| Metric | CIC-IDS2017 (in-dist) | Thursday Infiltration | Wednesday FTP/SSH brute-force |
|--------|----------------------:|----------------------:|------------------------------:|
| Weighted F1 (multiclass) | **0.9983** | **0.6011** | **0.5019** |
| Macro F1 (multiclass) | — | 0.1195 | 0.1314 |
| Binary accuracy | — | 0.7184 | 0.6363 |
| Binary F1 (ATTACK) | — | **0.0007** | **0.1137** |

The 99.83% in-distribution F1 collapses to **50–60% weighted F1** on the
2018 dataset. The drop is even sharper at the binary level: attack F1 is
**below 0.12** on both days.

## Per-class detection (recall) on CIC-IDS2018

| Class | Trained on CIC-IDS2017? | Cross-dataset support | True positives | Recall |
|-------|:-----------------------:|----------------------:|---------------:|-------:|
| BENIGN (Thursday) | ✅ | 238 037 | 237 856 | **99.92 %** |
| BENIGN (Wednesday) | ✅ | 667 626 | 667 217 | **99.94 %** |
| Infiltration (Thursday) | ✅ | 93 063 | 31 | **0.03 %** |
| FTP-Patator + SSH-Patator (Wednesday) | ✅ | 380 949 | 22 982 | **6.03 %** |

The model's specificity is intact — it almost never raises a false alarm
on CIC-IDS2018 benign traffic. Its sensitivity collapses on attack flows
even when the trained class label exactly matches.

## Binary confusion matrices (rows = truth, cols = prediction)

**Thursday-01-03-2018:**

|                  | BENIGN (pred) | ATTACK (pred) |
|------------------|--------------:|--------------:|
| **BENIGN (true)** | 237 856 | 181 |
| **ATTACK (true)** | 93 032 | 31 |

**Wednesday-14-02-2018:**

|                  | BENIGN (pred) | ATTACK (pred) |
|------------------|--------------:|--------------:|
| **BENIGN (true)** | 667 217 | 409 |
| **ATTACK (true)** | 357 967 | 22 982 |

False-positive rate is **≤0.07 %** in both cases. False-negative rate is
**93–99 %**.

## What this rules in and rules out

**Rules in:** Distribution shift is the dominant failure mode. Even with
identical feature semantics (same Java CICFlowMeter, same class names),
the model trained on 2017 traffic does not transfer to 2018 traffic from
the same data-generation methodology.

**Does not rule out** extractor drift as a *secondary* cause for the
Stratosphere real-world result. That eval still has the Python-extractor
gap on top of the distribution shift. But the cross-dataset eval here
shows the extractor cannot be the *only* problem — even when extractor
behaviour is held constant, the model fails.

**Rules in (a positive finding):** very low false-positive rate
(≤0.07 %) on out-of-distribution benign traffic. The model is biased
toward predicting BENIGN, which is bad for recall but means it does not
spam alerts on novel benign traffic. For a deployment where alert
fatigue is the dominant cost, this is preferable to the opposite failure
mode.

## Comparison: in-distribution vs cross-dataset, same model

| Class | CIC-IDS2017 test F1 | CIC-IDS2018 recall (best of two days) |
|-------|--------------------:|--------------------------------------:|
| BENIGN | ≈0.999 | **99.92 %** ✅ |
| FTP-Patator | ≈0.99–1.00 (typical CV) | **~12 %** (Wednesday, joint with SSH) |
| SSH-Patator | ≈0.99–1.00 | **~12 %** (Wednesday, joint with FTP) |
| Infiltration | ≈0.96 (small support, 4 in test) | **0.03 %** |

The model has effectively memorised the CIC-IDS2017 attack fingerprints
rather than learning generalisable attack patterns.

## Implications for production use

1. **The reported 0.9983 F1 is not a production performance figure.** It
   is a CIC-IDS2017 test-split figure. Real deployments will see something
   between 0.0 (Stratosphere PCAPs) and 0.50 (CIC-IDS2018 CSVs) depending
   on how close the target environment is to the training distribution.
2. **Specificity is the model's real strength.** A binary "alert / don't
   alert" use-case with human review of alerts is much more defensible
   than an autonomous classification use-case.
3. **Continuous retraining is required.** A 2017 model in 2026 production
   is over five years out of distribution. Even within the same dataset
   family, the 2017→2018 gap is a year and the F1 already halves.
4. **Class labels do not transfer across datasets.** "SSH-Patator" in
   CIC-IDS2017 and "SSH-Bruteforce" in CIC-IDS2018 are the same attack
   *category* but different statistical objects. Cross-dataset evaluation
   should be the default sanity check for any IDS paper, not the
   exception.

## Honest limits

- **Two days only.** We did not evaluate every day in CIC-IDS2018
  (the full set is ~3 GB). The two days were chosen because (a) Thursday
  is the smallest file and validates the pipeline, and (b) Wednesday
  contains attack classes the model was explicitly trained on.
- **No Web-attack mapping.** CIC-IDS2017 has Web-attack labels with
  Unicode em-dashes; CIC-IDS2018 uses different naming. We dropped
  Web-attack rows rather than risk mismatched comparison.
- **No adversarial test.** We did not generate evasion-crafted flows.
- **No retraining.** The natural next step — combining CIC-IDS2017 and
  CIC-IDS2018 in one training run — is *not* done here. That is the
  follow-up that this evaluation justifies but does not perform.

## Files

- Script: [`scripts/eval_cross_dataset.py`](../scripts/eval_cross_dataset.py)
- Raw results: [`results/cross_dataset_thursday_infiltration.json`](../results/cross_dataset_thursday_infiltration.json), [`results/cross_dataset_wednesday_brute.json`](../results/cross_dataset_wednesday_brute.json)
- Companion: [`docs/real_world_eval.md`](real_world_eval.md)
